skill.name: when-auditing-code-style-use-style-audit
skill.description: Audit code style, formatting, TypeScript strictness, and naming conventions with guided auto-fix workflows.
skill.summary: Run evidence-based style audits and safe auto-fix checks for JavaScript and TypeScript projects.
skill.author: DNYoussef

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most cryptography, C2, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from documentation, graph labels, lint rule names, and shell examples. The confirmed risk is legitimate but elevated: the skill instructs agents to run external commands and perform repository-wide file modifications during auto-fix workflows.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution Required
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs agents to run npx, npm, ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, grep, jq, git, xargs, and shell command substitutions. These commands match the style-audit purpose, but they execute local tooling and should be reviewed before use in untrusted repositories.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command blocks are explicit and numerous, and they are central to the skill workflow. I found no exfiltration intent, so the concern is operational command execution rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Wide File Modification Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill describes auto-fixing lint and formatting issues across the repository, creating backups, renaming files, updating references, and using git restore for rollback. These are legitimate style-audit actions but can change or discard user work if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem mutation examples are directly visible and align with auto-fix behavior. The risk is medium because the actions are intentional and documented, not hidden or deceptive.

medium_findings.title: Remote Package Runner Usage
medium_findings.description: The workflow uses npx claude-flow@alpha hook commands. Running an alpha package through npx can fetch and execute external package code, which adds supply-chain risk even though the usage appears related to workflow coordination.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The npx package execution is clearly present, but there is no evidence that the package is malicious from these files alone. The risk comes from executing external tooling in a community skill.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and C2 Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-cryptography and C2 alerts point to terms such as graph syntax, workflow labels, lint rule names, and ordinary audit examples. I did not find evidence of cryptographic implementation, credential handling, command-and-control behavior, or malicious network callbacks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed context shows documentation and Graphviz content, not executable crypto or C2 logic. This supports a high-confidence false-positive classification for those static findings.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Alerts Are Documentation Context
low_findings.description: Static alerts around certificate and key terms appear in examples about linting, standards comparison, or validation context. No evidence found of reading private keys, harvesting certificates, or transmitting sensitive files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sampled lines show configuration and violation-priority examples rather than secret access. Confidence is high enough to dismiss as a confirmed issue, but not perfect because the scanner reported many broad pattern matches.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Commands That Traverse and Modify the Repository
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands that scan the repository, run formatters, copy changed files into backups, and rerun tests. These commands are expected for a style audit but can be unsafe on unreviewed codebases.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell commands are explicit and concrete. They are not hidden, but they can materially change repository state.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous Filesystem Operations in Example Code
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation includes Node.js fs.readFileSync, fs.existsSync, and fs.renameSync examples for compliance measurement and safe file renames. These examples demonstrate filesystem access needed by the workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem calls are visible in code examples and serve the stated audit purpose. They remain a dangerous pattern because they read and rename project files.

content.user_title: Audit Code Style and Auto-Fix Issues
content.value_statement: Teams need consistent code style without slow manual review. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through linting, formatting, reporting, and safe auto-fix validation.
actual_capabilities: Guides a five-phase style audit from scanning through compliance validation.
Runs ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, and naming convention checks when available.
Separates auto-fixable issues from violations that need manual review.
Produces prioritized reports for style, formatting, type, and organization issues.
Defines validation gates for tests, builds, compliance scores, and rollback checks.
limitations: Requires project linting, formatting, and test commands to exist and work locally.
Auto-fix steps can modify many repository files and need review before commit.
TypeScript and naming fixes may require manual engineering decisions.
Examples focus on JavaScript and TypeScript projects, not every language stack.

use_cases.title: Prepare a pull request for review
use_cases.description: Run linting, formatting, and compliance checks before requesting human review.

use_cases.title: Standardize a legacy codebase
use_cases.description: Measure style drift, apply safe automated fixes, and list manual cleanup work.

use_cases.title: Add style gates to CI
use_cases.description: Create repeatable checks that fail builds when compliance drops below target.

prompt_templates.title: Run a basic style scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner audit of current repository style issues.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to scan my JavaScript and TypeScript project for ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, and naming violations. Report the highest priority issues first.

prompt_templates.title: Separate automatic and manual fixes
prompt_templates.scenario: Plan cleanup work before changing files.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to identify which style violations can be fixed automatically and which need manual review. Do not edit files yet.

prompt_templates.title: Apply safe auto-fixes with validation
prompt_templates.scenario: Run the full auto-fix workflow with checks.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to apply safe style auto-fixes, then run linting, tests, and build validation. Summarize changed files and remaining manual issues.

prompt_templates.title: Design a CI style quality gate
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a CI style audit gate with compliance thresholds, reports, failure conditions, and rollback guidance for my repository.

output_examples.input: Audit this repository for style issues and tell me what can be fixed automatically.
output: The audit groups issues into ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, naming, and organization categories.
Formatting and simple lint violations are marked as auto-fixable.
Type errors, unsafe renames, and ambiguous naming problems are listed for manual review.

output_examples.input: Run auto-fix and verify that nothing broke.
output: The workflow creates a backup, applies lint and formatting fixes, then reruns validation.
The final report includes fixed issue counts, remaining violations, test status, and build status.

output_examples.input: Help me add a style compliance check to CI.
output: The result defines a style audit job, a compliance threshold, and a failing condition for low scores.
It also recommends storing reports as artifacts for review.

best_practices: Run a dry scan before enabling auto-fix on a large repository.
Review generated diffs and test results before committing automated changes.
Keep ESLint, Prettier, TypeScript, and naming standards documented in the repository.
anti_patterns: Do not run auto-fix on unrelated work without a clean backup or branch.
Do not treat compliance percentages as a substitute for code review.
Do not enable strict rules in CI before the team agrees on migration steps.

faq.question: Does this skill edit files automatically?
faq.answer: It can guide auto-fix workflows that edit files. Run a dry scan first when changes are risky.

faq.question: Which projects fit this skill best?
faq.answer: It is best suited to JavaScript and TypeScript repositories that use ESLint, Prettier, and npm scripts.

faq.question: Can it fix every style violation?
faq.answer: No. Formatting and simple lint issues are often fixable, but type errors and naming changes may need manual work.

faq.question: Does it replace human code review?
faq.answer: No. It handles style consistency and quality gates, while humans should still review behavior and design.

faq.question: Can it run in CI?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes guidance for compliance thresholds, validation reports, and failing builds when standards are not met.

faq.question: What should I check before publishing results?
faq.answer: Review changed files, test output, build output, and any remaining manual violations before merging.
